title: Psychosomatic Medicine
abstract:
ments on page 1101, the investigators
listed a number of factors that might
have led to more striking results. One
of these was that the duration of the
lowering, of lipid levels in the study
might not have been long enough to
influence long-standing coronary ar-
tery disease.
The belief in the lipid hypothesis
has stimulated many public health
programs aimed at reducing
serum
cholesterol levels in hopes of affecting
the incidence of ischemic heart dis-
ease.
The patient monitoring
and
reassurance necessary to achieve good
patient response and cooperation in
controlling hyperlipidemia and the
other
cardiovascular
risk
factors
probably preclude
the community-
wide approach without the private
physician's assuming an active role
on behalf of his patients. While
a
beneficial effect on serum lipid levels
should be evident after three months
of clofibrate therapy (or the drug
therapy should be discontinued), any
treatment expected to show a benefi¬
cial effect on coronary heart disease
or atherosclerosis would of necessity
have to be long-term.
Barbara J. Leibowitz, MD
New York
1. Oliver MF, Heady JA, Morris JN, et al: A
co-
operative trial in the primary prevention of ischaemic
heart disease using clofibrate: Report from the Commit-
tee of Principal Investigators. Br Heart J 40:1069-1118,
1978.
2. Oliver MF: Cholesterol, coronaries, clofibrate and
death. N Engl J Med 299:1360-1361, 1978.
See also JAMA U2:656, 1979.—Ed.
Psychosomatic Medicine
To the Editor.\p=m-\Areview of the book
by
G\l=u"\nterAmmon, Psychoanalysis
and Psychosomatics (241:1939, 1979),
notes that "in 1922
one of Freud's
followers, Felix Deutsch, a Viennese
psychiatrist, seemed set to unravel
the secrets of mind-body interaction
when he coined the term psychoso-
matic." It was, in fact, Johann Chris-
tian Heinroth (1773-1843), author of
Lehrbuch der Stoerungen des Seelen-
lebens, who is generally credited with
having coined the term "psychoso-
matic medicine."1
Heinroth was concerned with men-
tal illness as a disease of the soul,
with sin as a cause of disease, and
with psychotherapy, classification of
mental illness,
and the history of
these subjects. He first used the term
"psychosomatic" in 1818, but psycho-
somatic medicine as a field of scien-
tific study
and
clinical
evaluation
clearly
developed
most intensively
only during the past few decades,
having come into special prominence
after 1935, the publication date of
Dunbar's2 survey. Ackerknecht3 com-
ments understandably that Heinroth,
despite his contributions to psychia-
try, cannot be regarded as "one of the
fathers of psychosomatic medicine."
The original coining of "psychoso¬
matic" had been forgotten, so that in
1943 Weiss and English4 referred to
"psychosomatic medicine" as "a new
term." Yet also in that year, the
introduction to another book by Dun-
bar5 incorporated
a quotation from
William
Osier's
widely
read
The
Principles and Practice of Medicine,
in which the term "psychosomatic
medicine" appeared.
Osier died in
1919.
Jerome M. Schneck, MD
St Vincent's Hospital and
Medical Center
New York
1. Schneck JM: A History of Psychiatry. Springfield,
Ill, Charles C Thomas Publisher, 1960, p 100.
2. Dunbar F: Emotions and Bodily Changes: A Survey
of Literature on Psychosomatic Interrelationships. New
York, Columbia University Press, 1935.
3. Ackerknecht EH: A Short History of Psychiatry.
New York, Hafner Publishing Co, 1959, p 53.
4. Weiss E, English
OS: Psychosomatic Medicine.
Philadelphia, WB Saunders Co, 1943, p 1.
5. Dunbar F: Psychosomatic Diagnosis.
New York,
Paul B Hoeber Inc, 1943,.p 1.
Use of a Teaching Pneumatic
Otoscope
To the Editor.\p=m-\Conventional instruc-
tion in otoscopy relies on sequential
observations of the tympanic mem-
brane by instructor and student. This
method
is
beset
with
difficulties,
including discomfort for the patient,
trauma
to
the
external
auditory
canal, and the inability of the instruc-
tor to be
sure that the student is
seeing the structures described. Par-
ticularly difficult to teach with con-
ventional
methods
is the skill of
pneumatic otoscopy. Students must
learn the response of the tympanic
membrane to varying pressures of
air. This mode of sequential examina-
tion trains students poorly; interob-
server differences are great.1 Teach-
ing operating microscopes used by
otolaryngologists obviate these diffi-
culties, but such devices
are large,
expensive, and usually unavailable in
a pediatric clinic or office.
At our request,
a teaching pneu-
matic
otoscope
was
designed
and
manufactured by Welch-Allyn Inc,
Skaneateles Falls, NY (Figure). Two
viewing
ports
allow
examination
simultaneously by two observers. The
instructor
can demonstrate specific
structures, show the response of the
tympanic membrane to varying air
pressure,
and verify
the
student's
accuracy in diagnosis of disease of the
middle
ear. The teaching otoscope
produces the second image by use of
Double-headed teaching otoscope.
an image-splitting mirror. Thus, the
image in the side port is reversed. The
side port rotates to any convenient
angle, and the optics allow observa¬
tion of the middle ear without requir¬
ing the examiners' eyes to be close to
the lens. The otoscope fits a standard
battery handle and thus is readily
portable.
Acute otitis media afflicts a major¬
ity of children by their second birth¬
day2; such children require antimicro¬
bial agents and continued observation
until resolution of the disease of the
middle
ear.
For
many,
resolution
comes slowly. Many other children
suffer from persistent, asymptomatic
effusions of the middle ear. Accumu¬
lating evidence suggests that persist¬
ent fluid in the middle ear may lead
to substantial delay in development of
speech and language
or to impair¬
ment of cognitive functions.35 Thus,
pediatricians and other practitioners
who care for children must be able to
diagnose accurately fluid in the mid¬
dle ear. We have used the teaching
pneumatic
otoscope
extensively
to
instruct medical students, house staff
officers, and pediatricians in practice
in the examination of the middle ear
as well as to assess their diagnostic
acumen. We find it to be a valuable
instrument for these tasks.
David W. Teele, MD
Jerome O. Klein, MD
Boston City Hospital
Boston University School
of Medicine
This investigation was supported in part by contract
NOI AI 52536 from the National Institute of Allergy and
Infectious Diseases.
